title: Distributions & the Bell Curve
description: Binomial, normal, CLT — and why real markets have fatter tails than the textbook.

levels.title: Binomial World
intro: Count successes in n independent trials with success probability p: that's Binomial(n, p). P(k successes) = C(n,k)·pᵏ·(1−p)ⁿ⁻ᵏ. Mean np, variance np(1−p).
The complement trick: P(at least one) = 1 − P(none). It turns nasty sums into one-liners.

exercises.prompt: Probability of exactly 2 heads in 4 fair coin flips? (decimal)
exercises.explanation: C(4,2) = 6 ways, each with probability (1/2)⁴ = 1/16, so 6/16 = 0.375. Counting from Unit 1 powering the binomial.

exercises.prompt: A strategy wins each of 100 independent trades with probability 0.3. Expected number of wins?
exercises.explanation: Binomial mean = np = 100 × 0.3 = 30. Linearity of expectation again: each trade contributes 0.3 wins in expectation.
np(1−p) = 100 × 0.25 × 0.75 = 18.75, so SD ≈ 4.33 wins. Variance is largest when p = 0.5 — maximum uncertainty.

exercises.prompt: Which distribution models the number of attempts UNTIL your first fill (success) at an exchange?
exercises.explanation: Binomial counts successes in a FIXED number of trials; geometric counts trials until the FIRST success. You met its mean (1/p) in Unit 2.

exercises.prompt: Chevalier de Méré's bet: probability of at least one 6 in four die rolls? (3 decimals)
exercises.explanation: 1 − (5/6)⁴ ≈ 1 − 0.482 = 0.518. Just over half — de Méré got rich on this before probability theory even existed.

levels.title: The Normal
intro: The normal (Gaussian) is the bell curve: fully described by mean μ and SD σ. Rule of thumb: ~68% of mass within 1σ, ~95% within 2σ, ~99.7% within 3σ.
The Central Limit Theorem says sums/averages of many independent effects look normal — that's why it shows up everywhere. Standardize with z = (x − μ)/σ.

exercises.prompt: For a normal distribution, roughly what fraction of outcomes fall within 1 standard deviation of the mean?
exercises.explanation: 68 / 95 / 99.7 for 1σ / 2σ / 3σ. Burn this in — it's the fastest sanity check in all of quant work.

exercises.prompt: IQ scores: mean 100, SD 15. What is the z-score of 130?
exercises.explanation: z = (130 − 100)/15 = 2. Z-scores convert any normal into the standard one so a single table (or intuition) fits all.

exercises.prompt: Approximate probability a normal variable lands MORE than 2 SDs above the mean?
exercises.explanation: 95% is within ±2σ, so 5% is outside, split evenly: ~2.5% (2.28% exactly) in the upper tail alone.

options: Sums of many independent variables tend toward a normal distribution
exercises.explanation: Whatever the individual distribution (finite variance), the sum/average of many independent copies approaches normal. Daily returns ≈ sum of many small shocks — hence the bell shape (mostly...).

exercises.prompt: A stock's daily return has SD 2%. Assuming independent days, what is the SD of the 9-day return, in %?
exercises.explanation: Variances add for independent days: 9 × 4 = 36 → SD = 6%. Risk scales with √time (√9 × 2%) — the square-root-of-time rule.

levels.title: Tails & Reality
intro: Here's the punchline practitioners live by: real market returns are NOT normal. Extreme moves happen far more often than the bell curve predicts — 'fat tails'.
Sigma-counting is still the common language: a '5-sigma day' means a move 5 SDs from the mean. Under normality that's near-impossible; markets deliver them anyway.

exercises.prompt: A stock's daily moves have SD 1%. Today it dropped 5%. How many sigmas is that?
exercises.explanation: z = 5%/1% = 5 sigma. Under a normal distribution that's about a 1-in-3.5-million day — once in ~14,000 trading years.

exercises.prompt: Markets produce 5-sigma days every few years, not every 14,000 years. Best conclusion?
options: Returns have fat tails — the normal model understates extremes
exercises.explanation: Real return distributions have excess kurtosis: more mass in the tails (and the peak) than a Gaussian. Models that assume normality die on exactly these days — see 1987, 2008, 2020.

exercises.prompt: Daily return SD is 1.5% and there are 252 trading days. Annualized volatility in %? (1 decimal)
exercises.explanation: 1.5% × √252 ≈ 1.5 × 15.87 ≈ 23.8%. Same √time rule you used last level, scaled to a year.

exercises.prompt: You hold two assets with correlation +1.0. How much risk reduction does this diversification give?
options: None — perfectly correlated assets are effectively one asset
exercises.explanation: Diversification works through imperfect correlation. At ρ = 1 the portfolio SD is just the weighted average — no cancellation. Worse: in crashes, correlations tend to spike toward 1 exactly when you need diversification most.

exercises.prompt: Which portfolio has the LOWEST variance? Two assets, each SD 10%, weights 50/50, with correlation:
exercises.explanation: At ρ = −1 the assets cancel perfectly: portfolio variance hits zero. This is the theoretical dream behind hedging — Unit 6 makes it concrete.
